title: Present perfect: basic form
question: 'I ___ finished my homework.' Which is correct?
explanation: Use 'have' with I/you/we/they to form the present perfect: 'I have finished.'

title: Present perfect: he/she
question: 'She ___ visited Paris three times.' Which is correct?
explanation: With he/she/it, use 'has' for present perfect: 'She has visited.'

title: Present perfect: past participle of 'try'
question: 'Have you ever ___ sushi?' Which is correct?
explanation: The past participle of 'try' is 'tried' (change y to i, add -ed). 'Have you ever tried sushi?'

title: Present perfect: past participle of 'be'
question: 'I have ___ to Australia, but I'd love to go one day.' Which is correct?
explanation: The past participle of 'be' is 'been'. After 'have', use 'been', not 'was', 'am' or 'being'.

title: Present perfect: already
question: 'They have ___ left the building.' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Already' goes between 'have' and the past participle. It means 'before now' or 'sooner than expected.'

title: Present perfect: yet
question: 'The train hasn't arrived ___.' Which is correct?
explanation: Use 'yet' at the end of negative sentences and questions. It means 'up to now': 'hasn't arrived yet.'

title: Present perfect: past participle of 'eat'
question: 'We have just ___ lunch.' Which is correct?
explanation: The past participle of 'eat' is 'eaten' (irregular). After 'have/has', use the past participle: 'have eaten'.

title: Present perfect: negative
question: 'I ___ seen that film.' (negative) Which is correct?
explanation: The negative of 'have' in present perfect is 'haven't' (for I/you/we/they): 'I haven't seen.'

title: Present perfect: question form
question: '___ you ever met a famous person?' Which is correct?
explanation: Present perfect questions start with 'Have/Has': 'Have you ever met...?'

title: Present perfect: past participle
question: 'She has ___ three books this year.' Which is correct?
explanation: After 'has/have', use the past participle. The past participle of 'read' is 'read' (irregular verb).

title: Present perfect vs past simple: time word
question: 'I ___ to London last summer.' Which is correct?
explanation: Use past simple (went) with a finished time word like 'last summer.' Not present perfect.

title: Present perfect: past participle of 'write'
question: 'She has ___ two emails today.' Which is correct?
explanation: The past participle of 'write' is 'written' (irregular). After 'has', use 'written', not the past simple 'wrote'.

title: Present perfect vs past simple: ago
question: 'We ___ married ten years ago.' Which is correct?
explanation: Use past simple with 'ago': 'We got married ten years ago.' Never use present perfect with 'ago.'

title: Present perfect vs past simple: experience
question: '___ you ever seen a shooting star?' Which is correct?
explanation: Use present perfect with 'ever' to ask about life experiences: 'Have you ever seen...?'

title: Present perfect vs past simple: yesterday
question: 'He ___ his keys yesterday.' Which is correct?
explanation: Use past simple with 'yesterday' because it is a finished time: 'He lost his keys yesterday.'

title: Present perfect: auxiliary agreement
question: 'They ___ visited the museum this week.' Which is correct?
explanation: With 'they', use 'have' to form the present perfect: 'They have visited'. 'Has' is only for he/she/it.

title: Present perfect: past participle of 'lose'
question: 'Oh no, I have ___ my keys! I can't get in.' Which is correct?
explanation: The past participle of 'lose' is 'lost' (irregular). 'I have lost my keys' means they are still missing now.

title: Past simple question: 'when'
question: 'When ___ you start learning English? Last year?' Which is correct?
explanation: Use 'did + subject + base verb' for past simple questions: 'When did you start...?'

title: Modals: can - ability
question: 'She ___ speak three languages.' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Can' is a modal verb. It doesn't change form: 'She can speak.' No 's', no 'to.'

title: Modals: can - permission
question: '___ I use your phone?' Which is correct?
explanation: Use 'Can I...?' to ask for permission. Modal verbs don't add -s and don't combine with 'am' or 'do'.

title: Modals: can't - inability
question: 'I ___ swim. I never learned.' Which is correct?
explanation: The negative of 'can' is 'can't' (cannot). No other words needed: 'I can't swim.'

title: Modals: could - past ability
question: 'When I was young, I ___ run very fast.' Which is correct?
explanation: Use 'could' for ability in the past: 'When I was young, I could run fast.'

title: Modals: could - polite request form
question: 'Could you ___ me, please?' Which is correct?
explanation: After a modal verb like 'could', use the base form of the verb: 'Could you help me?' — no 'to', no -ing, no -s.

title: Modals: must - obligation form
question: 'You ___ wear a seatbelt. It's the law.' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Must' expresses strong obligation. It's a modal verb: no 'to', no -s, and no 'be' before it.

title: Modals: must - strong advice
question: 'It's a great film. You ___ see it!' Which is correct?
explanation: Use 'must' for very strong recommendations: 'You must see it!' (= I strongly recommend it.)

title: Modals: mustn't
question: 'You ___ park here. It's not allowed.' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Mustn't' means 'it is not allowed / it is forbidden.' 'You mustn't park here.'

title: Modals: should - advice
question: 'You look tired. You ___ go to bed early.' Which is correct?
explanation: Use 'should' to give advice: 'You should go to bed.' It's softer than 'must.'

title: Modals: shouldn't
question: 'You ___ eat so much sugar. It's bad for you.' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Shouldn't' is the negative of 'should.' Use it for advice about what is not a good idea.

title: Modals: may - possibility
question: 'It ___ rain later. Take an umbrella.' Which is correct?
explanation: Use 'may' to talk about something that is possible: 'It may rain' = maybe it will rain.

title: Modals: may - permission form
question: '___ I sit here?' Which is correct?
explanation: 'May I...?' is a formal way to ask for permission. Modal verbs don't add -s, don't take 'to', and don't combine with 'am'.

title: Modals: must + base verb
question: 'You must ___ this report before 5 pm.' Which is correct?
explanation: After 'must' (and all modals), use the base form of the verb: 'must finish'. No 'to', no -ing, no -ed.

title: Modals: should vs must
question: 'You ___ try the chocolate cake here. It's delicious.' Which is correct?
explanation: Use 'should' for friendly recommendations. 'Must' is stronger. Modal verbs don't need 'to.'

title: Future: will - decision now
question: 'I'm hungry. I ___ make a sandwich.' Which is correct?
explanation: Use 'will' for decisions made at the moment of speaking: 'I'll make a sandwich.'

title: Future: will - promise
question: 'I promise I ___ call you tonight.' Which is correct?
explanation: Use 'will' for promises: 'I promise I will call you.' No 'to' after 'will.'

title: Future: will - form
question: 'I think it ___ be sunny tomorrow.' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Will' is a modal verb. It doesn't add -s, doesn't take 'to', and is followed by the base form: 'will be'.

title: Future: will - negative
question: 'She ___ come to the meeting. She's too busy.' Which is correct?
explanation: The negative of 'will' is 'won't' (will not): 'She won't come.'

title: Future: will - question
question: '___ you be at the party tonight?' Which is correct?
explanation: Questions with 'will': 'Will + subject + base verb?' 'Will you be at the party?'

title: Future: going to - form with 'we'
question: 'We ___ going to visit our grandparents next weekend.' Which is correct?
explanation: The 'going to' future uses am/is/are. With 'we', use 'are': 'We are going to visit.'

title: Future: going to - form with 'it'
question: 'Look at those dark clouds. It ___ going to rain.' Which is correct?
explanation: The 'going to' future uses am/is/are. With 'it', use 'is': 'It is going to rain.'

title: Future: going to - question
question: 'What ___ you going to do this evening?' Which is correct?
explanation: Questions with 'going to' use am/is/are: 'What are you going to do?'

title: Future: going to - negative
question: 'I ___ going to buy that car. It's too expensive.' Which is correct?
explanation: Negative of 'going to': am/is/are + not. 'I am not going to buy that car.'

title: Future: going to - form with 'I'
question: 'I ___ going to study medicine at university.' Which is correct?
explanation: The 'going to' future uses am/is/are. With 'I', use 'am': 'I am going to study medicine.'

title: Future: will - form with 'I'
question: Someone knocks at the door. 'I ___ get it!' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Will' is a modal verb. It doesn't add -s, doesn't take 'to', and doesn't combine with 'am': 'I will get it!'

title: Present perfect: for + duration
question: 'We ___ lived in this house for ten years.' Which is correct?
explanation: With 'we', use 'have' for present perfect. 'For ten years' shows a duration continuing to now: 'We have lived here for ten years.'

title: Present perfect: since
question: 'He has worked here ___ 2015.' Which is correct?
explanation: Use 'since' with a point in time (2015, Monday, April). Use 'for' with a duration (ten years, two months).

title: Present perfect vs past simple: just
question: 'You're too late! The film ___ just finished.' Which is correct?
explanation: Use present perfect with 'just' for something that finished a very short time ago. With 'the film' (he/she/it), use 'has': 'The film has just finished.'

title: Modals: couldn't - past inability
question: 'Yesterday I ___ finish my homework because I was very tired.' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Couldn't' is the negative past of 'can'. With 'yesterday' (finished past time), use 'couldn't': 'I couldn't finish my homework.'

title: Modals: may not - not allowed (formal)
question: 'Students ___ use phones during the exam.' Which is correct?
explanation: 'May not' is the negative of 'may' for permission. Modal verbs don't combine with 'do' and don't take 'to'.

title: Future: going to - plan
question: 'I've decided. I ___ study French next year.' Which is correct?
explanation: Use 'going to' for plans decided before speaking. After 'I', use 'am going to' — the 'be' verb is required.

title: Future: will - base form after
question: 'The weather forecast says it ___ snow tomorrow.' Which is correct?
explanation: After 'will', use the base form of the verb: 'will snow'. 'Will' never adds -s and never takes 'to'.
